prompt: Tell me a bio of Gerhard Fischer
evaluation_criteria: Factual accuracy of profession -- commonly described as 'inventor' but he actually commercialized (not invented) the metal detector. Verify patent date (1931), nationality, and specific contributions to metal detection technology. The distinction between inventor and commercializer is a critical factual nuance.
output:
There are several notable people named **Gerhard Fischer**. Here are the four most prominent:

---

## 1. Gerhard Fischer — Diplomat & Humanitarian (1921–2006)
**Born:** 20 September 1921, Oslo, Norway  
**Died:** 3 July 2006, Copenhagen, Denmark  

A German diplomat, ambassador, and humanitarian who received the **1997 Gandhi Peace Prize** for his work with leprosy and polio patients in India. Born in Oslo to a Norwegian mother and German father (sinologist Martin Fischer), he grew up in China and began studying medicine at Beijing Medical University, where he first volunteered with leprosy patients. The Japanese occupation forced him to abandon his studies. He traveled to Germany, was compelled to fight in WWII (mainly on the Eastern Front), and spent nearly 3 years as a POW in France.

After the war, he earned a law degree in just one year and joined the German foreign service in 1952. His postings included Addis Ababa, Hong Kong, Madras, and Bonn. He served as West German Ambassador to **Malaysia** (1970–74), **Ireland** (1977–80), the **Netherlands** (1980–83), and **Switzerland** (1983–85). While consul in Madras, he helped establish the **Indian Institute of Technology Madras** and supported a leprosy treatment center at Chettipatty.

In 1985, he resigned from the diplomatic service just six months before retirement to devote himself full-time to leprosy and polio patients in India. He established health centres, workshops, and schools throughout India, emphasizing rehabilitation and vocational training. He insisted patients never be called "lepers" and would touch every patient without gloves or masks, saying *Nalla thane irukku* ("You are all right"). He used the Gandhi Peace Prize money (10 million rupees) to set up a foundation. His daughter Karen Fischer Koch continued his work after his death.

---

## 2. Gerhard Fischer — Inventor (1899–1988)
**Born:** 18 March 1899, Landeshut, Silesia (then Germany)  
**Died:** 2 May 1988, Palo Alto, California, USA  

A German-American entrepreneur and inventor who pioneered the **hand-held metal detector**. Born in Silesia, his family moved to Dresden around 1906. He studied electrical engineering at Dresden University, then emigrated to the United States. While working with aircraft radio direction finders, he noticed that environmental metal caused interference — leading him to realize the technology could detect metal objects. He shared the idea with **Albert Einstein**, who predicted the widespread use of metal detectors. In **1931**, he founded **Fisher Research Laboratory, Inc.** in Palo Alto and patented his metal detector in **1937**. He led the company until his retirement in 1967.

---

## 3. Gerhard Fischer — Computer Science Professor (born 1945)
**Born:** 2 July 1945  

**Professor Emeritus of Computer Science** at the University of Colorado Boulder, Fellow of the Institute of Cognitive Science, and founder/director of the **Center for LifeLong Learning & Design (L3D)**. He earned a Master's from Heidelberg University (1971), a PhD from the University of Hamburg (1977), and did postdoctoral work at **MIT** (with Seymour Papert) and **Xerox PARC** (with Alan Kay). His habilitation advisor was **Herbert A. Simon** (Nobel laureate).

His research spans **human-computer interaction, meta-design, lifelong learning, cultures of participation, and intelligence augmentation**. He served as principal advisor to 20 PhD students. Awards include: **ACM Fellow** (2009), **SIGCHI Academy** (2007), **RIGO Award** (SIGDOC, 2012), honorary doctorate from the University of Gothenburg (2015), and the **EUSSET-IISI Lifetime Achievement Award** (2025).

---

## 4. Gerhard Fischer — Architect & Archaeologist (1890–1977)
**Born:** 12 July 1890, Bergen, Norway  
**Died:** 10 September 1977, Oslo, Norway  

A Norwegian architect and archaeologist, son of architect Adolph Fischer. He studied in Bergen, Oslo, and Copenhagen. From 1916–1926, he designed railway stations for the Norwegian State Railways (including Lillestrøm, Notodden, Åneby, and Ljan stations). From 1938–1960, he was a conservator at the University's Antiquities Collection in Oslo, leading excavations of medieval sites including Sverresborg, Bergenhus Fortress, Utstein Abbey, and Stavanger Cathedral. He authored several books on Norwegian historical architecture. Awards include the **Medal of St. Hallvard** (1956) and **Commander of the Order of St. Olav** (1965).

---

Which Gerhard Fischer were you looking for? I'd be happy to provide more detail on any of them.
